blocks.text:
If z is a function of x and y, i.e., z = f(x, y), the total differentials of z are obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following rule:

$$dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy.$$

The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of slope, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$. 

Thus:

blocks.latex: \text{or total change in } z = \Delta z = \Delta x \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} + \Delta y \frac{\partial z}{\partial y}.
blocks.description: This equation illustrates the total change in the function z when there are changes in both x and y.

blocks.text:
The change in z due to a change in x can be measured by the product of the change in x multiplied by the rate at which z is referencing to a diagram (Fig. 21). 

Let P be a point (x, y) on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P. The change of position is most easily expressed as a vector on the surface (y is kept constant).
